g.data-narration: Higher Transition Probabilities in Markov Chains - computing probabilities for n steps into the future
g.text: Higher Transition Probabilities in Markov Chains
Computing probabilities for n steps into the future

g.data-narration: One-step probabilities tell us about the next step, but what about weather in three days or stock prices in thirty days?
g.text: Why Do We Need Multi-Step Predictions?
One-step probabilities (P_ij) tell us what happens in the NEXT step. But what about
the weather in 3 days? Stock prices in 30 days? That's where n-step transition
probabilities come in — they predict the future over multiple transitions.

g.data-narration: The n-step transition probability equals the probability of moving from state i to state j in exactly n steps
g.text: This is the probability of moving from state i to state j in EXACTLY n steps.
For homogeneous Markov chains, it depends only on n, not on the starting time m.

g.data-narration: The Chapman-Kolmogorov equations let us break an n-step journey into two parts via an intermediate state k
g.text: The fundamental tool for computing n-step probabilities: break an n-step journey into
two parts: from state i to intermediate state k (r steps), then from k to j (n-r steps).
P_ij^(n) = Sum over k of P_ik^(r) * P_kj^(n-r)

g.data-narration: The useful form shows probability from i to j in m plus n steps equals sum of products of shorter transitions
g.text: The most useful form: P_ij^(m+n) = Sum over k of P_ik^(m) * P_kj^(n)

g.data-narration: The n-step transition matrix is simply the n-th power of the one-step matrix, computing all probabilities at once
g.text: The n-step transition matrix is simply the n-th power of the one-step matrix!
The (i,j) entry of P^n gives P_ij^(n) — compute all probabilities at once.

g.data-narration: In our weather example with Sunny and Rainy states, the transition matrix shows probabilities between weather states
g.text: Two states: Sunny (S) and Rainy (R). Today's weather determines tomorrow's weather.
S to S: 0.8 | S to R: 0.2 | R to S: 0.3 | R to R: 0.7

g.data-narration: Using Chapman-Kolmogorov, P_SS^(2) equals the sum of two paths: sunny to sunny twice plus sunny to rainy then rainy to sunny
g.text: Find P_SS^(2): probability it's sunny 2 days from now, given sunny today.
P_SS^(2) = P_SS x P_SS + P_SR x P_RS
Two paths: S to S to S AND S to R to S

g.data-narration: Computing all two-step probabilities gives this matrix showing chances of each weather combination after two days
Method two uses matrix multiplication: P squared equals P times P, giving the same two-step transition probabilities
g.text: Multiply P by itself to get P^(2):

g.data-narration: Both methods give the same result, confirming P_SS squared equals 0.70. Higher powers give longer-term predictions
g.text: P_SS^(2) = 0.70 from BOTH methods - confirming correctness
Compute higher powers for more steps: P^5, P^10 for long-term predictions.

g.data-narration: As n increases, the matrix powers converge toward a steady-state where each row becomes identical for long-term forecasting
g.text: As n increases, P^n approaches a steady-state matrix where each row is identical.

g.data-narration: Three key insights: Chapman-Kolmogorov breaks paths, matrix power is efficient, one calculation gives all probabilities

g.text: Break n steps into shorter paths
P^n = P x P x ... x P

g.data-narration: These methods apply to Google PageRank, predictive text, financial modeling, and disease spread forecasting
To summarize: n-step probabilities predict future states, Chapman-Kolmogorov provides the mathematical foundation, and matrix powers offer the most efficient computation
g.text: - n-step probability P_ij^(n) equals probability of going from i to j in n transitions
- Chapman-Kolmogorov: P_ij^(n) equals Sum over k of P_ik^(r) times P_kj^(n-r)
- Matrix method: P^(n) equals P to the power n - the most efficient computational approach
- Powers converge to steady-state for long-term predictions
